source.title: An Evolution-Based Approach to De Novo Protein Design and Case Study on Mycobacterium tuberculosis

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Computational protein design aims to create new sequences with desired structure and function. Existing physics-based methods (PBM) suffer from force field inaccuracies and cannot reliably recognize protein folds, especially for medium-to-large proteins. The hypothesis is that using evolutionary information, in the form of structural profiles from evolutionarily related protein families, can guide sequence design more robustly, analogous to how homology modeling improves structure prediction. The goal is to develop an evolution-based method (EBM) that combines structural profiles with physics-based terms to enhance foldability and biological functionality.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: Developed a novel evolution-based protein design method (EBM) that uses structural profiles from evolutionarily related proteins to guide sequence search, combined with physics-based packing. Tested on 87 proteins via computational folding (I-TASSER), achieving average RMSD 2.1 Å, significantly better than physics-based methods. Applied to redesign all 243 structurally resolved M. tuberculosis proteins. Experimental validation on five designed proteins showed all soluble with secondary structure; three had ordered tertiary structure by NMR.
process_signals: Method inspired by success of homology modeling in structure prediction
Combines evolution and physics terms
Computational validation shows improved foldability
Large-scale application to pathogen proteins
Wet-lab validation confirms some designs fold correctly
methods: Structural profile construction from evolutionarily related proteins
Monte Carlo sequence search guided by profile and physics terms
Experimental validation by CD and NMR
metrics: TM-score: not explicitly in abstract but implied high
failures_or_limits: Two out of five experimentally tested proteins lacked stable fold by NMR

section_gists.heading: Introduction
section_gists.gist: Protein design is reverse of folding. Homology modeling is most reliable for structure prediction, using sequence profiles. Physics-based ab initio folding works poorly for larger proteins due to force field inaccuracy. Paradoxically, physics-based design has had some success, but designed proteins are often less well-defined than natural ones. Proposed evolution-based method to constrain sequence search with structural profiles, akin to threading, to improve fold recognition and design robustness.
process_signals: Motivation: physics-based force fields inaccurate for fold recognition
Hypothesis: evolutionary profiles can guide design like they guide structure prediction
Previous work: physics-based designs often structurally less defined
Some success with physics-based design due to iterative search reinforcing target match
failures_or_limits: Physics-based ab initio folding limited to small proteins (<100 residues) with RMSD>3-5 Å
Force field inaccuracy is major obstacle
Designed proteins often less stable/defined than natural ones

section_gists.heading: Author Summary
section_gists.gist: Most design methods use physics-based force fields following Anfinsen's hypothesis, but force field inaccuracy is a major obstacle. Proposed method uses evolutionary information (sequence profiles from structure families) to guide design, targeting a family of profiles to enhance robustness. Tested on 87 proteins, designed sequences folded by I-TASSER to average RMSD 2.1 Å. Large-scale application to M. tuberculosis. Five sequences experimentally characterized: all soluble with secondary structure, three with ordered tertiary structure.
process_signals: Problem: force field inaccuracy in physics-based methods
Solution: use evolutionary profiles to constrain design
Advantage: targets a family of profiles, enhancing robustness
Validation: computational folding and experimental testing
methods: Evolution-based protein design using structural profiles
failures_or_limits: Two designs lacked stable fold by NMR

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: Design procedure: structural profile construction and profile-guided Monte Carlo search, with final sequence selected by clustering. Test set: 87 proteins from PISCES (resolution ≤1.6 Å, sequence identity ≤30%), length 52-197 residues, covering SCOP classes (17 α, 22 β, 14 α/β, 32 α+β, 2 small).
process_signals: Test set selection criteria: high resolution, low sequence identity, globular folds
Design pipeline: profile construction → Monte Carlo search → clustering

section_gists.heading: Rational of folding simulations for protein design verifications
section_gists.gist: Used I-TASSER for computational validation because experimental validation is expensive and ab initio methods limited. I-TASSER can fold medium-to-large proteins without homologous templates, with high success rate (~3/4). Previous study showed I-TASSER selective to native-like sequences: randomized sequences with same secondary structure propensity could not be folded below 6 Å, while well-designed sequences 77% below 2 Å. Tested on 45 previous design sequences (16 folded, 29 unfolded) and showed I-TASSER C-score and estimated TM-score/RMSD correlate well with actual quality, distinguishing folded from unfolded designs.
process_signals: Choice of I-TASSER justified by its ability to fold without homologs and selectivity for native-like sequences
Validation of I-TASSER as a foldability discriminator using known folded/unfolded designs
Correlation between C-score and actual TM-score/RMSD established
methods: I-TASSER folding simulations with homologous template exclusion
Benchmark on 45 previous designs (16 folded, 29 unfolded)
failures_or_limits: Two failed EBM designs on MDM2 included in unfolded set (not folded by CD)

section_gists.heading: I-TASSER based 3D structure prediction of designed sequences
section_gists.gist: Compared EBM and PBM on 87 proteins. EBM: average RMSD 2.12 Å, TM-score 0.87; PBM: RMSD 4.14 Å, TM-score 0.74. EBM: 80% RMSD<2.0 Å, 42.5% <1.0 Å; PBM: 54% <2.0 Å, 31% <1.0 Å. PBM TM-scores bimodal (very high or very low), EBM more consistent. For 14 cases where PBM TM-score<0.3, EBM improved to >0.75 except one (2ZXY_A: 0.20 to 0.69). EBM designs closer to scaffold than to analogs (TM-score 22.5% higher). Sequence identity: EBM 28%, PBM 21%. Control: artificial sequences with same identity but random residues gave RMSD ~9.7 Å, showing identity alone not sufficient. Even with conserved residues copied and non-conserved matched by type, folding not as good.
process_signals: EBM significantly outperforms PBM in foldability
PBM unreliable: some designs fold well, others fail completely
Evolutionary profiles rescue failed PBM designs
Designs converge to scaffold center due to consensus effect
Higher sequence identity alone does not explain improved folding
methods: I-TASSER folding with PSI-BLAST homolog exclusion
failures_or_limits: PBM fails on many cases (bimodal distribution)
One case (2ZXY_A) only improved to TM-score 0.69 by EBM

section_gists.heading: Secondary structure assignment
section_gists.gist: Developed PSSpred (Q3=84.5%) to assess secondary structure. EBM NRE=0.33, seven times lower than PBM (2.40). Example CD59: PBM missed three strands, introduced long helix; Q3: EBM 96%, PBM 53%. EBM's better SS due to profile smoothening and SS energy terms improving short-range cooperation.
process_signals: EBM preserves secondary structure much better than PBM
Physics-based force field alone fails to capture secondary structure cooperation
Profile and SS energy terms crucial for correct secondary structure

section_gists.heading: Backbone torsion angle and solvation assignments
section_gists.gist: EBM NRE for Φ, Ψ, SA: 0.14, 0.22, 0.02; PBM: 0.43, 1.01, 0.41. EBM errors 3.1, 4.6, 20.5 times lower. Example CD59: SA correlation 0.74 with EBM, 0.42 without profile. Torsion errors mainly in loops but also in regular secondary structure, affecting folding.
process_signals: EBM significantly improves local structural features
Profile restraints crucial for correct torsion and solvation
Errors in torsion angles can disrupt folding stability
methods: Neural network predictors for Φ, Ψ, SA

section_gists.heading: Target sequence recapitulation
section_gists.gist: EBM sequence identity 28% (core 41%), PBM 21% (core 35%). EBM recapitulation comparable to Kuhlman (27%) but lower than Saunders (37%). Despite low identity, high structural similarity. EBM better recapitulates conserved residues: 32% identical vs 23% for PBM; 44% highly homologous vs 29%. Conserved residues often near functional sites. Profiles help recognize evolutionarily conserved residues critical for fold and function.
process_signals: Low sequence identity does not prevent correct folding
EBM better captures evolutionarily conserved positions
Conserved residues important for function and fold
metrics: Highly homologous in conserved: EBM 44%, PBM 29%
failures_or_limits: Sequence recapitulation lower than some other methods

section_gists.heading: Amino acid composition
section_gists.gist: EBM composition deviation 1.1%, PBM 3.4%. PBM over-enriches hydrophobic residues (Met, Pro) due to FoldX bias, which may disrupt secondary structure. EBM has even distribution.
process_signals: Physics-based potential biases amino acid composition towards hydrophobic
EBM avoids this bias, resulting in more native-like composition
methods: Core/surface classification by relative accessible surface area <0.16
failures_or_limits: PBM over-enrichment of Met and Pro may disrupt secondary structure

section_gists.heading: Number of structures needed for structural profile construction
section_gists.gist: Default TM-score cutoff 0.7 for analogs; if <10 analogs, reduced cutoff to 0.6 or 0.5. EBM performance depends on analog quality: RMSD 1.46 Å (cutoff>0.7, 41 proteins), 2.57 Å (>0.6, 24), 2.84 Å (>0.5, 22). PBM shows opposite trend due to length differences. Excluding all analogs with TM-score>0.5 increased EBM RMSD to 2.66 Å, but still better than PBM. Torsion and SS also depend on cutoff; solvation and sequence identity do not. PDB nearing completeness, so analogs usually exist.
process_signals: EBM performance depends on availability of close structural analogs
Even distant analogs (TM-score>0.5) improve over pure physics-based design
Parameter tuning: TM-score cutoff adjusted to ensure at least 10 analogs
PDB completeness mitigates concern for novel folds
methods: Structural analog search with TM-score cutoffs
Ablation: excluding all analogs with TM-score>0.5
metrics: Without analogs >0.5: RMSD 2.66 Å, NRE SS 2.11, Φ 0.6, Ψ 0.99
failures_or_limits: Some targets require lowering cutoff to 0.5

section_gists.heading: Protein design based on the evolution terms only
section_gists.gist: EvBM (evolution-only) compared: TM-score 0.82, RMSD 2.82 Å, vs EBM 0.87/2.12 and PBM 0.74/4.14. EvBM NRE: SS 0.48, Φ 0.26, Ψ 0.30, SA 0.04. Sequence identity 27% (core 35%). EvBM outperforms PBM significantly, close to EBM, showing evolution terms are major driving force.
process_signals: Ablation study: evolution terms alone perform nearly as well as combined
Physics terms provide incremental improvement
Evolutionary information is the key innovation
methods: EvBM: design using only evolutionary energy terms (no FoldX)
metrics: RMSD: EvBM 2.82 Å, EBM 2.12 Å, PBM 4.14 Å

section_gists.heading: A case study on M. tuberculosis proteins
section_gists.gist: Applied EBM to all 243 solved MTb proteins (length up to 296). Average NRE: SS 0.29, Φ/Ψ 0.14/0.18, SA 0.09. Average RMSD 3.28 Å (higher due to larger proteins; excluding >200 residues gives 2.57 Å). Binding site prediction with COFACTOR: 62% of designs have binding partners (51.3% enzymes), slightly more than targets (59%). Designed proteins have more binding sites (6.2 vs 5.5) and higher binding affinity in 75% of common sites. Examples: thioredoxin C gained a sulfate-binding pocket; PZAase conserved zinc-binding residues but H71 flipped.
process_signals: Large-scale application demonstrates scalability
Performance consistent with benchmark
Designed proteins may gain new binding functionalities
Binding site conservation even without explicit design constraints
methods: EBM pipeline automated for large-scale design
metrics: 75% common sites have higher affinity in design
failures_or_limits: Higher RMSD for larger proteins due to I-TASSER limitations
Some binding site details not perfectly recapitulated (e.g., H71 flip)

section_gists.heading: Experimental validation of five EBM designed proteins
section_gists.gist: Five proteins selected: hnRNPK (1ZZK), thioredoxin (1R26), CISK-PX (1XTE), LOV2 (2V0U), TIF1 (3I4O). Length 68-146, RMSD 1.33-2.99 Å. All expressed and soluble. CD showed all have secondary structure consistent with targets. NMR: thioredoxin, CISK-PX, TIF1 have stable tertiary folds; hnRNPK and LOV2 do not. Urea unfolding: thioredoxin ΔG = -16.1 kJ/mol (incomplete unfolding at 9.5 M), CISK-PX ΔG = -29.6 kJ/mol (unfolds by 8.5 M).
process_signals: Experimental validation confirms computational predictions partially
Success: 3/5 have stable folds
Failure: 2/5 lack stable tertiary structure despite soluble and secondary structure
Thermodynamic stability measured for two
methods: Urea denaturation for folding free energy
failures_or_limits: hnRNPK and LOV2: no stable fold by NMR
Thioredoxin unfolding incomplete at 9.5 M urea
TIF1 not tested for unfolding due to low 222 nm signal

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The research evolved from recognizing the limitations of physics-based design (force field inaccuracy, poor fold recognition) to hypothesizing that evolutionary profiles, successful in structure prediction, could guide sequence design.
Initial design combined evolution and physics terms (EBM), tested computationally against PBM, showing significant improvement.
Ablation study (EvBM) confirmed that evolution terms are the primary driver, with physics terms providing incremental benefit.
Parameter sensitivity analysis (number of analogs) revealed dependence on structural analog availability, but even distant analogs help.
Large-scale application to M. tuberculosis demonstrated scalability and potential for functional design (binding sites).
Experimental validation showed that computational success does not guarantee stable folds in vitro (2/5 failures), indicating remaining challenges in design robustness.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Why do some designs (hnRNPK, LOV2) fail to fold in vitro despite good computational metrics?
What are the specific limitations of the energy function or search that lead to these failures?
How does the method perform on truly novel folds with no analogs even at TM-score 0.5?
Can the method be improved by incorporating more accurate physics-based terms or better profile construction?
What is the success rate in larger experimental validations?
Are the designed proteins functional in vivo?

events.summary: Physics-based force fields are inaccurate for protein fold recognition, limiting design of medium-to-large proteins.
Using evolutionary information from structure families can guide sequence design more robustly than physics-based methods.
Developed an evolution-based protein design method (EBM) combining structural profiles with physics-based packing.
I-TASSER C-score and estimated TM-score/RMSD correlate well with actual quality, distinguishing folded from unfolded designs.
Five designed sequences were experimentally validated; all soluble with secondary structure, three with ordered tertiary structure.
Two out of five experimentally tested proteins lacked stable fold by NMR.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers abstract, introduction, author summary, and early results including method overview, test set, validation rationale, and experimental outcomes. Events extracted reflect problem statement, hypothesis, method design, computational validation, and experimental results with limitations.

events.summary: I-TASSER folding validation on 45 previous design sequences shows correlation between estimated and actual TM-score/RMSD, distinguishing folded from unfolded designs.
EBM designed sequences show improved foldability over PBM in I-TASSER folding simulations, with average RMSD 2.12 Å vs 4.14 Å.
PBM TM-score distribution is bimodal (very high or very low), indicating unreliability; EBM improves TM-scores for all 14 low-scoring PBM cases except one.
EBM designs are closer to scaffold than to structural analogs (TM-score 22.5% higher), suggesting consensus effect drives design toward center structure.
numbers_or_metrics: TM-score to scaffold 22.5% higher than to analogs

events.summary: EBM secondary structure NRE is 0.33, seven times lower than PBM (2.40), due to profile smoothening and SS energy terms improving short-range cooperation.
numbers_or_metrics: Q3 accuracy: EBM 96%, PBM 53% for CD59

events.summary: EBM backbone torsion and solvation NREs are significantly lower than PBM, indicating better local structural features.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains detailed computational validation results comparing EBM and PBM, including foldability, secondary structure, torsion angles, and control experiments.

events.summary: Analysis of secondary structure error reduction in EBM due to profile smoothening and SS energy terms.
Dependence of EBM performance on number of structural analogs and TM-score cutoff.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains detailed analysis of EBM vs PBM performance on secondary structure, torsion angles, solvation, sequence recapitulation, amino acid composition, dependence on structural analogs, ablation study (EvBM), and case study on M. tuberculosis.

events.summary: Comparison of EvBM (evolution-only) with EBM and PBM shows evolution terms are the major driving force.
Large-scale application of EBM to redesign all 243 solved M. tuberculosis proteins.
Performance on MTb proteins consistent with test set; higher RMSD due to larger proteins.
Ligand-binding prediction shows 62% of designed MTb proteins have binding partners, slightly more than targets.
numbers_or_metrics: 6.2 binding sites per design vs 5.5 per target
75% common sites have higher affinity in design

events.summary: Example: thioredoxin C design gains a sulfate-binding pocket not present in target.
Example: PZAase design conserves zinc-binding residues but H71 flips; Y132I mutation eliminates sulfate binding.
Experimental validation of five EBM-designed proteins: all soluble with secondary structure; three have stable tertiary fold by NMR.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers ablation study (EvBM), large-scale MTb application, binding site analysis, and experimental validation with failures.

events.summary: NMR spectroscopy reveals that hnRNPK and Lov2 designs lack stable fold, while thioredoxin, CISK-PX, and TIF1 show stable folds.
Urea denaturation experiments determine folding free energies for thioredoxin and CISK-PX designs.
Discussion highlights that EBM reduces normalized relative errors significantly compared to PBM: 7 times for secondary structure, 3-5 times for torsion angles, 21 times for solvent accessibility.
I-TASSER folding of EBM sequences achieves average RMSD 2.12 Å to target structures, indicating captured essential fold features.
EBM recapitulates evolutionarily conserved residues better than PBM: 44% vs 23% identity in conserved regions.
EBM reduces amino acid composition deviation from native to 1.1%, compared to 3.4% for PBM, and eliminates Proline bias.
Large-scale application to 243 M. tuberculosis proteins yields average RMSD 3.28 Å (2.57 Å for proteins ≤200 residues).
In 75% of cases with confident binding partners, EBM designs show higher binding affinity than targets.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains experimental validation results (NMR, urea denaturation), discussion of EBM improvements over PBM, and large-scale application analysis.

events.summary: The energy function for Monte Carlo search combines evolutionary terms (structural profile match, secondary structure, solvent accessibility, torsion angles) and physics-based terms (FoldX force field).
Final designed sequence is selected by clustering all sequence decoys from ten independent Monte Carlo runs, using BLOSUM62 distance and SPICKER-like algorithm.
The clustering method relates cluster size to free energy, so the largest cluster corresponds to the lowest free-energy state.
Metropolis Monte Carlo simulations can be trapped at local minima, as shown by low pairwise sequence identity (28%) between lowest energy sequences of different runs.
Clustering improves design quality: average RMSD of I-TASSER models from clustered sequences is 1.5 Å lower than from lowest energy sequences of individual runs.
numbers_or_metrics: RMSD 3.7 Å vs 2.2 Å (implied 1.5 Å improvement)

events.summary: Computation time is high when including FoldX (16.5 hours) due to side-chain calculation by SCWRL, compared to 3.8 hours without FoldX.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk details the design methodology, energy function, parameterization, control experiments, clustering selection, and limitations of the Monte Carlo approach.
This chunk contains only reference citations and acknowledgments, with no research evolution events.

evidence_bank.quote: the inaccuracy of the force-field design, which cannot accurately describe atomic interactions or correctly recognize protein folds
We propose a novel method which uses evolutionary information, in the form of sequence profiles from structure families, to guide the sequence design.
The outline of our protein design procedure is shown in Figure 1, which consists of structural profile construction and the profile-guided Monte Carlo sequence space searching simulation.

the estimated TM-score and RMSD of the I-TASSER predictions for the designed proteins are highly correlated with the actual TM-score and RMSD, with a correlation coefficient of 0.91 and 0.80, respectively.

five sequences were randomly selected from the design pool and subjected to experimental validation. The results showed that all the designed proteins are soluble with distinct secondary structure and three have well ordered tertiary structure
three have well ordered tertiary structure, as demonstrated by circular dichroism and NMR spectroscopy
the average TM-scores (RMSDs) are 0.718 (3.9 Å) and 0.551 (6.8 Å), respectively
the RMSD and TM-score for the EBM designed sequences are 2.12 Å and 0.87, respectively, which demonstrated an improved foldability by EBM
the distribution of TM-scores of the PBM designed sequences is more divergent, i.e. the TM-scores are either very high (>0.85) or very low (<0.35)
the TM-score of the EBM design to the scaffold is 22.5% higher than the TM-score to the structural analogs in the profile
The NRE of designed sequence by the EBM is 0.33, which is seven times lower than that by the PBM method
The NRE of designed sequence by the EBM is 0.33, which is seven times lower than that by the PBM method

The reduction in the secondary structure error for the EBM method is mainly due to the smoothening effects introduced by the structural profiles and the single-sequence based SS energy terms, which significantly increase the short-range residue cooperation that are usually missed in the physics-based force fields.
the average RMSD of the I-TASSER models for the first group of 46 proteins is 1.46 Å, which is lower than the two other groups based on the lower TM-score cutoffs (2.57 Å and 2.84 Å).
the TM-score of the I-TASSER models on the EvBM design is 11% higher than those created by PBM
the evolution-based energy terms, including the profiles and the knowledge-based structure feature predictions, are the major driving force for the designs conducted by the EBM pipeline
we apply the pipeline to redesign all solved proteins in M. tuberculosis (MTb) genome
243 distinct proteins with length up to 296 residues have had their protein structure solved
The average RMSD of the I-TASSER models is, however, relatively higher (=3.28 Å)
If we exclude the proteins of length >200 residues, the average RMSD of the I-TASSER models is reduced to 2.57 Å
62% of the EBM designed proteins have binding partners with a high confidence score
COFACTOR detects slightly less (59%) binding partners
the EBM designed sequences have on average more binding sites (6.2 per protein) than the target proteins (5.5 per protein)
mutations of T35S, G38P, and S79G on the designed sequence change the local binding pocket conformation and therefore facilitate the formation of four hydrogen bonds with the sulfate ion
the designed protein shows remarkable conservation within these regions
the configuration of H71 is flipped in the model
all the designed domains successfully expressed and were soluble

the hnRNPK and Lov2 designs lacked upfield methyl chemical shifts (-1.0-0.5 ppm) and had sparse features in the protein amide range (5.5–10.0 ppm) (Figures 7F and 7I), which suggest that they do not possess a stable fold.

the designs for the thioredoxin, CISK-PX and TIF1 domains showed strongly shielded methyl shifts between 0.5 and -1.0 ppm and had well-resolved peaks in the amide region, features that are indicative of proteins possessing stable folds.
Free energies of folding for the thioredoxin and CISK-PX domains were calculated by linear regression from the data points available and determined to be -16.1 and -29.6 kJ/mol, respectively [76].
the normalized relative errors are reduced by 7 times for secondary structure, 3 (5) times for $\Phi(\Psi)$ angles, and 21 times for solvent accessibilities.
an average RMSD of 2.12 Å is achieved to the target structures although all homologous templates detectable by PSI-BLAST were excluded from our predictions (Table 1).
the identity of residues in the evolutionarily conserved regions is much higher (i.e. 44%; in comparison, only 23% residues are conserved in these regions in the PBM designed sequences).
the absolute composition difference from the native sequence is reduced by more than three times (from 3.4% to 1.1%), and the bias of designed sequence to Proline is completely eliminated in EBM.
The designed sequences can be folded by I-TASSER to the structures of average RMSD 3.28 Å without using homologous templates (or 2.57 Å for the proteins below 200 residues).

In 75% of the cases where there is a confident binding partner on the same binding site, the binding affinity is higher in the EBM proteins than in the target, as shown by the binding free-energy calculated by COFACTOR [66] and X-score [69].
The energy function of the MC search consists of two parts. The first part counts for the alignment match of the sequence decoy with the target structural profile
The second part of the energy function contains a physics-based force field from FoldX V3.0b5
For each target, ten independent Monte Carlo runs, each starting from a different random sequence, are performed.
The final designed sequence is selected by clustering all the sequence decoys generated in Monte Carlo simulations.
the design sequences with the largest cluster size in EBM should correspond to the state of the lowest free-energy in our simulations
the pair-wise sequence identity between the lowest energy sequences of ten different runs is low (28% on average)
the average RMSD of the I-TASSER models on the ten lowest energy sequences from the ten MC runs is 3.7 Å, 1.5 Å higher than that obtained for sequences selected from clustering
the average simulation time of our method without using FoldX is 3.8 hours, while including FoldX increases the time for the program to 16.5 hours
Among the 18 residues whose side-chains are involved at the active site, five have been mutated in the EBM design (V23I, A95G, E101L, A102R, and Y132I).

Interestingly, although no natural binding pocket exists in the target, mutations of T35S, G38P, and S79G on the designed sequence change the local binding pocket conformation and therefore facilitate the formation of four hydrogen bonds with the sulfate ion (dashed line in Figure 6B) that was identified by COFACTOR as the binding ligand.
Nevertheless, one of the two competing binding sites (the one with sulfate ion) was eliminated by the mutation of Y132I in which one aromatic hydrophilic residue is replaced by a hydrophobic residue.

All the mutations are spatially clustered together with other active site residues, except for Y132I where one aromatic hydrophilic residue is exchanged for a hydrophobic residue (yellow in Figure 6F).
Y132I mutation in EBM design is designated by yellow.
(2010) Discovery of entry inhibitors for HIV-1 via a new de novo protein design framework.
A final purification polishing step by size-exclusion gel filtration using a (GE) AKTA chromatographic work station and an S-100 column was conducted.
In the designed protein, the latter was completely eliminated, mainly due to the aromatic exchange at Y132I.
MTb proteins are encoded by 4,062 genes where 243 distinct proteins with length up to 296 residues have had their protein structure solved in the PDB library $[65]$ .

In reality, recent studies have demonstrated that the current PDB library is approaching to completeness and almost all the proteins, including random homopolypeptides, can have protein analogs with similar folds (TM-score>0.5) in the PDB [62–64].

We found that 32% of residues in the conserved positions in the EBM sequences are identical to that in the target and 44% of residues in the conserved regions are highly homologous (with a BLOSUM62 mutation score>0.5) to the corresponding residues in the target.
Saravanan KM, Balasubramanian H, Nallusamy S, Samuel S (2010) Sequence and structural analysis of two designed proteins with 88% identity adopting different folds.
Guerois R, Nielsen JE, Serrano L (2002) Predicting changes in the stability of proteins and protein complexes: a study of more than 1000 mutations.
These sequences have a length ranging from 68 to 146 residues and cover different fold types.
These proteins contain different fold types (4 αβ- and 1 β-proteins) with length ranging from 68 to 146 residues.

To examine the impact of the mutations and the aromatic exchange on overall ligand binding, we ran COFACTOR based on the I-TASSER model of the designed protein and the target structures, which identified one isochorismic acid binding site (Figure 6F; space filled atoms) including Residue-132 for both proteins with a binding affinity 5.1 pKd as calculated by X-Score.

We found that 32% of residues in the conserved positions in the EBM sequences are identical to that in the target and 44% of residues in the conserved regions are highly homologous (with a BLOSUM62 mutation score>0.

To evaluate the likelihood of the designed sequences to fold into stable and desired structures (or the foldability), we exploited the I-TASSER pipeline $[33–34]$ to generate structural models for each of the designed sequences and then examine the structural similarity to the target scaffold, where all homologous templates to the scaffold sequence were excluded from the threading structure library.
Wang R, Lai L, Wang S (2002) Further development and validation of empirical scoring functions for structure-based binding affinity prediction.
Skolnick J, Zhou HY, Brylinski M (2012) Further Evidence for the Likely Completeness of the Library of Solved Single Domain Protein Structures.
Zhang Y, Skolnick J (2005) The protein structure prediction problem could be solved using the current PDB library.
Chen H, Zhou HX (2005) Prediction of solvent accessibility and sites of deleterious mutations from protein sequence.
Wei Y, Kim S, Fela D, Baum J, Hecht MH (2003) Solution structure of a de novo protein from a designed combinatorial library.
(2012) De novo peptide design with C3a receptor agonist and antagonist activities: theoretical predictions and experimental validation.
(2010) New compstatin variants through two de novo protein design frameworks.
(2006) IPRO: an iterative computational protein library redesign and optimization procedure.
(2003) Design of a novel globular protein fold with atomic-level accuracy.
7 Å, 1.5 Å higher than that obtained for sequences selected from clustering (see Table 1), which demonstrates the necessity of multiple MC runs and the usefulness of sequence clustering.
The current EBM programs implemented 10 independent Metropolis MC runs, each running 30,000 mutation movements.
The parameters $f_{1-9}$ were trained by maximizing the correlation between the calculated and experimental free-energy changes on a set of experimental residue mutants.

For a given target protein structure (or scaffold), the profile is constructed from a family of structural analogs that are collected from a non-redundant set of the PDB library by the structural alignment program, TM-align [77], using the scaffold as the probe.

Two typical examples were shown in Figure 6: for thioredoxin, a new binding pocket was formed by the mutation of three key residues in the active site, which improved the binding pocket shape and hydrogen-bonding network with the ligand; for PZAase, although the overall sequence identity is only 39%, the triad, cis-peptide and metal ion binding residues at the active site
The method is tested on the design experiment of 87 non-homologous single-domain proteins covering different fold classes.
5–10.0 ppm) (Figures 7F and 7I), which suggest that they do not possess a stable fold.

To examine the impact of the mutations and the aromatic exchange on overall ligand binding, we ran COFACTOR based on the I-TASSER model of the designed protein and the target structures, which identified one isochorismic acid binding site (Figure 6F; space filled atoms) including Residue-132 for both proteins with a binding affinity 5.

The average RMSD of the I-TASSER models is, however, relatively higher (=3.28 Å); this is mainly due to the difficulty of I-TASSER in folding large proteins since all homologous templates have been excluded from the template library.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Physics-based force fields are inaccurate for protein fold recognition, limiting design of medium-to-large proteins.

canonical_events.summary: Physics-based force fields are inaccurate for protein fold recognition, limiting design of medium-to-large proteins.
Using evolutionary information from structure families can guide sequence design more robustly than physics-based methods.
Developed an evolution-based protein design method (EBM) combining structural profiles with physics-based packing.
I-TASSER folding validation on 45 previous design sequences shows correlation between estimated and actual TM-score/RMSD, distinguishing folded from unfolded designs.
EBM designed sequences show improved foldability over PBM in I-TASSER folding simulations, with average RMSD 2.12 Å vs 4.14 Å.
PBM TM-score distribution is bimodal (very high or very low), indicating unreliability; EBM improves TM-scores for all 14 low-scoring PBM cases except one.
EBM secondary structure NRE is 0.33, seven times lower than PBM (2.40), due to profile smoothening and SS energy terms improving short-range cooperation.
EBM backbone torsion and solvation NREs are significantly lower than PBM, indicating better local structural features.
Dependence of EBM performance on number of structural analogs and TM-score cutoff.
Comparison of EvBM (evolution-only) with EBM and PBM shows evolution terms are the major driving force.
key_numbers: RMSD: EvBM 2.82 Å, EBM 2.12 Å, PBM 4.14 Å

canonical_events.summary: Large-scale application of EBM to redesign all 243 solved M. tuberculosis proteins.
Performance on MTb proteins consistent with test set; higher RMSD due to larger proteins.
Ligand-binding prediction shows 62% of designed MTb proteins have binding partners, slightly more than targets.
key_numbers: 6.2 binding sites per design vs 5.5 per target

canonical_events.summary: Experimental validation of five EBM-designed proteins: all soluble with secondary structure; three have stable tertiary fold by NMR.
Two out of five experimentally tested proteins (hnRNPK and LOV2) lacked stable fold by NMR.
Urea denaturation experiments determine folding free energies for thioredoxin and CISK-PX designs.
Computation time is high when including FoldX (16.5 hours) due to side-chain calculation by SCWRL, compared to 3.8 hours without FoldX.

edges.reason: Problem of force field inaccuracy motivates hypothesis to use evolutionary information.
Hypothesis leads to development of EBM method.
EBM method requires validation of I-TASSER as foldability discriminator.
Validated I-TASSER used to compare EBM and PBM foldability.
Detailed analysis of TM-score distribution reveals PBM unreliability.
Secondary structure analysis shows EBM improvement.
Torsion and solvation analysis shows EBM improvement.
Parameter sensitivity analysis on number of analogs refines understanding of EBM performance.
Ablation study (EvBM) refines understanding of evolution vs physics contributions.
EBM method applied to large-scale MTb redesign.
EBM method enables experimental validation of selected designs.
Experimental validation reveals failure cases despite computational success.
Urea denaturation further characterizes successful designs.
Computational cost limitation of EBM method.

must_cover_points: Validation of I-TASSER as foldability discriminator
Computational comparison of EBM vs PBM (RMSD, TM-score)
Ablation study (EvBM) showing evolution terms are major driver
